---
title: "[Your project title]"
author: "[Your name]"
date: last-modified
---

<!-- The structure below matches the Week 8-9 writing workshop:
     opening question, corpus note, finding, limits, appendix.
     Replace every bracketed placeholder. Target 800-1,500 words. -->

## [The opening question]

[One or two paragraphs. The question that made you collect this corpus, stated so a
reader who has never met the data understands why it matters.]

## The corpus

[The distilled Data Biography, about a paragraph: where the data comes from, who is in
it, who is missing, what it cannot tell you, and the licensing tier it sits in.]

## [The headline finding]

[State the finding in one sentence, then show it. Insert your main chart here and walk
the reader through it: what to look at, and what it means.]

![[Chart caption: what the chart shows, in plain words]](images/main-chart.png)

[If you have an image finding, present it the same way.]

## How this could be wrong

[The robustness section, from Week 8: which check you ran (the shuffle test, the
re-run-without-a-slice, the compared-to-what), what survived, and where a reasonable
reader could still disagree.]

## Show your work (appendix)

[Your method choices and moments of doubt: what you cut, what surprised you, what the AI
got wrong, and how you caught it.]

**How the AI was used.** [Two or three sentences, published with the essay: what the
assistant wrote (the code, throughout), what it labeled and how you audited it, and what
it got wrong that you caught. Your interpretation and prose are your own; say so.]

**The notebook.** Every number and chart above is produced by
[the project notebook](https://colab.research.google.com/YOUR-NOTEBOOK-LINK), which runs
top to bottom without edits.

**Data and credit.** [Dataset name and link; the license it carries; anyone whose work
you built on.]
